sessions.details: Recorded 1980-12-03, first TX 1980-12-19. No known commercial release.

track_listing.details: LP-What A Bunch Of Sweeties; Polydor

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well hello there, this is TV on the Radio here, Thomas the Vance, and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Right, tonight we repeat two quite recent Rock Show sessions.
One by a band from the Midlands called Chevy, and one by a bunch of lords from Ulster who go by the name of Sweet Savage and make an excellent noise.
And we've got some new stuff by Pat Travers, by Praying Mantis, Wistbone, Ash, Status Quo, High Treason, Tigers of Pantang, and Saxon.
And some classics from Spirit, The Pink Fairies, Led Zeppelin, Rainbow, among others.
Not forgetting our Friday Night Connection, the answer to last week's and our new one.
But this evening, we start with this.
Absolute Magic, from an album as yet unreleased in this country.
The new album by Pat Travers, it has the title Radioactive.
It's going to come out on the Polydor label, that track was called New Age Music.
you might have recognized it at one time it was a single for inner circle great track and before
that you heard a classic from rush from the album permanent waves the track was called the spirit
of radio as always loads of people want to hear rush including this lot that's alex who comes
from cold wind bay wants to be remembered to nigel mergen i think it is a possibly morgan dave turner
pete craven red maynard tim alan jai and everybody in the lower sixth who are loonies
Pete Standing, Nick Swinglehurst and Simon Smith and all the rock experts in Chester demand a mention, courtesy of John Sampson of Chester.
Good evening to Rob Fish and Beacon Teeth and Fiona and Dean and Gary and Kath and all the headbangers in Colchester.
Best wishes from Moose, who lives in Forest Road in Colchester.
Sally Ladley, near Rugby in Warwickshire, wants me to tell Malcolm Pugh in Bristol that she misses him.
Hundreds of names on this.
Just let me say that Helen P. of Sherbourne High, the third year,
says hello to all her mates.
Gotta mention Tracy and Louise and Marianne and Hammy and Helen and Jackie
and Tumpf and Ped and Jellybean, give us one,
and Mary Mag and Owen and Ged and Porgy and all the hippies and heavies.
Best wishes to everybody at the Trinity High, Renfrew in Scotland.
And now here's the first of four, a repeat session by Sweet Savage.
I was really disappointed when I heard their single,
which is incidentally on that album, their version of All Day and All the Night.
I thought it was absolutely duff
And a really bad choice for a single
But I'm happy to say that their album sounds good
At least the rest of it does
That was Praying Mantis from their new album
Which is out on Arista
The album has the title Time Tells No Lies
That track was flirting with suicide
It's 24 and a half minutes after ten
Before that you heard the first of four
From our repeat session by Sweet Savage
The track was called Into The Light
The Praying Mantis track was four
Andy and John and Forbes
And Mark and Bibby and Ian and Mike C
And Mike Ass and Joke and Glynnis
as well as everybody else I've forgotten to mention, says Alan Kidd of Maybowl in Asia.
Portman Headbangers, Vince, Charlie W, Simon and Nick,
and Andy at Bruton, who's got a birthday sometimes, usually about once a year, right?
And Ted, and everybody at All Hallows, and Nick at Carver's, I think it is,
and Eddie at Winchester, steak all that, is the mention.
From Josh White, I think it is, name for a singer, I suppose.
Charlie Osborne, who live in Bryanston, in Blanford, in Dorset.
Carolyn Zool of Everton-in-Hance wants me to say hello to her old man,
which you just have to.
And now here's something which I'm playing because I want to play it
and because it's been asked for.
In fact, it's a segue of two tracks which are really old.
All programmes, any form of entertainment comes in for some criticism.
And this one is coming in for a bit of criticism at the moment
based on the fact that we don't play enough old tracks.
Well, we've only got about an hour and 45 minutes worth of music per week to fit it all in,
and it's a very, very difficult decision to know exactly what to put into this programme.
And probably you're right, we don't play enough old tracks.
But for people who like old tracks, I think you'll enjoy this one and the one that follows.
The first is by Spirit, the second by the Pink Fairies.
Right on, fight on from an album called What a Bunch of Sweeties,
the Pink Fairies that was from years and years ago.
Before that you heard Spirit from the best of Spirit, I played it.
The track was Dark-Eyed Woman.
Big Ferry's track was asked for by Jerry C. of Hackney in London.
Once he played for all the Hackney heavies,
Mickey, Robin, Tony, Bones, Digger,
G'day Digger, Steve Casey, and all the freaks from the Greengate,
and most especially for a lady by the name of Pam.
The Spirit track I played for Virginia Hope,
who lives at number 30, Skeddymore Road, Cranhill in Glasgow,
who wants me to say hello to her husband, John, who's a Saxon freak.
Saxon is in the show somewhere.
A little bit later on, nice to hear those two tracks again
Especially that Pink Ferris track
I remember when they were signed to the record label
That they were on, followed all these
To drive all the executives up the wall
25 and a half minutes to 11 on the Friday Rock Show
Here's Chevy
That's the other side of the double A-sided single
That's out by Diamondhead at the moment
That's the side that has the title played loud
Prior to that you heard
Chevy and Skybird
Went down to see Crocus at the Hammersmith Odeon
The other day, met some nice people
in and around the bar, as usual,
because I'm always in the bar in between the acts.
Well, nowhere else to go, is there, really?
And also some nice people in the auditorium as well,
including Nigel and Ian Webster,
who wanted me to say hello to all the lads and the lasses in Bedford.
Gentleman by the name of Stuart Henderson
lives at No. 7 Norbury Close Chandler's Ford.
Or at least I think he did,
because it seems today he's moving to another house,
and he's gone all out to get the stereo fixed by 10 o'clock tonight
so that he can listen to the Friday Rock Show.
Stuart Henderson, I hope your house is good to you.
Also, I'd like to dedicate a piece of music.
How about the one we just played by Diamond Head?
To Dave Madison.
Dave will be 27 on the 17th of March.
He lives at 13 Mulliner Close in Bucknell, Stoke-on-Trent, Staffordshire.
He'll say hello to his wife, Jan.
Watch it, Jan.
Son, Shane, who is nine, also on March 17th.
Happy birthday.
Oh, it's St. Patrick's Day.
Oh, bitchy.
Also, Elsie needs a mention.
And this nice card came from Jeanette and Mick Holford.
Here's a really good new single
It's coming out on the 20th of March
It's by Wishbone Ash
Incredible, plodding, sort of plodding
drum style of the late John Bonham
on that
That was Kashmir from the albums
Physical Graffiti by Led Zeppelin
Especially for Lorraine and Julie
A couple of nice ladies I sat next to
at the Crocus concert at the Hammersmith Odeon the other day
Zeppelin track was also for
Paul and Andy and Margaret and brother Bill
who's recently been in hospital
Bill is the brother of Christine who lives in Mecklesfield
in Cheshire. Must mention
all the headbangers of the
fifth year at Keele School. Best wishes
from Finlay Munro.
Kath Axford
of I think it's Brinidore
in Gwent. A well-wisher
once you're back.
Karen Page has recently been in hospital as well.
She was in corridor two, room seven at St. John
and St. Elizabeth Hospital, just round the corner from
where I live, strangely enough, in London
NW8. She sends best wishes
to all the nurses and doctors there. Thanks them very
much for the attention and wants me to say
at a Julie Butterfield who goes to St Paul's School in Maidstone, I think it is.
And also somebody demanded that I play something that featured Mr Jimmy Page,
which of course that track did.
That somebody was Craig Fullerton of Stonehaven in Scotland.
Must tell you of a treat that happened to me the other night.
Went to see Jeff Beck on Tuesday night.
Magic concert, one of the best I've ever seen in my life.
And guess who got up for the encore?
Jimmy Page.
And before that you heard the new single by Wishbone Ash.
It's coming out on the 20th of March.
It's called Underground.
And this is Sweet Savage.
track from the new album by status quo it's called never too late for them it never is the
track was called don't stop me now well that's something from our repeat session by sweet savage
it was called queen's vengeance seven minutes after 11 the friday rock show time for the friday
night connection the tracks we played you last week were take the long way home from breakfast
in america by super tramp she's gone by daryl hall and john oats from abandoned luncheonette
and the second setting for The Last Supper from 10cc,
featured on the album called Original Soundtrack.
I think I saw about three wrong answers in all the mail that we got this week.
Well done.
And the answer is breakfast in America,
abandoned luncheon,
and last supper, meal times.
Who got it right, I'll tell you.
Virtually everybody, I've told you that,
but the person who's going to get the £10 record token is
Miss Gail Winslade,
who lives at 323 Hythe Road,
Willsborough, Ashford-in-Kent.
She's a hard-working teacher, I believe,
so therefore deserves the £10 record token.
She's put together her own Friday Night Connection,
three bits of music that have a definite theme or connection
running therein.
Start concentrating now.
The Night Connection was put together by Gail S. Winslade,
who lives in Willsborough, Ashford-in-Kent.
If you think you know what The Connection is,
jot it down on a postcard, letter,
any form of communication you'd like to employ.
Send it to the Friday Night Connection,
the Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1,
London, W1A4WW.
Send your answer to the Friday Night Connection,
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
The winner gets a £10 record token.
And don't forget, please,
to put together your own Friday Night Connection as well.
21 and a half minutes after 11,
here's a very serious letter from Mr. Guy Steele
of Meadowbank, Gloucester Street, Newenting, Gloucester.
He writes this way.
Dear Tommy, Tony and the Dog,
the answer for the Friday Night Connection of the week,
the 21st of March 1981, is as follows.
The lead guitarist of the first band
had tea with the mother of the second band's vocalist
at the same time as the keyboard player of the second band
was embarking on a flight to see the first cousin
twice removed of the bass player of the third group.
The first band's mascot, a Siamese cat,
had previously bitten the stray hamster.
taken in by the grandfather of the author of the biography of the second group's producer,
who was, incidentally, a famous actor.
This actor's publisher had, two weeks before, been taken ill with food poisoning
after eating at the same restaurant as the rat which lived in the dustbin,
which was collected by the same dustbin who'd given artificial respiration
to the child of the third group's vocalist.
You're slacking, Tommy. That was so easy.
Now, his connection is as follows.
Yellow Submarine by Pinky and Perky, live.
Halfway Up The Stairs by Kermit's Mate
and Smurf's Christmas by Motorhead.
But he wants me to give you the answer right now.
The answer is that all the bands drink tea.
Brain ache, I think you deserve a £5 record token for that
and you can have this as well.
It's Chevy and rock on.
I'm told they come from Luton.
I've never heard of them before.
They're called High Treason.
I got that particular record because a gentleman by the name of...
It's Richard.
I can't read Richard's second name at all
but it's Richard who lives in Clifton, Sheppard in Bedfordshire.
Send me their single, it's on the Burlington label
And it's by this band called High Treason
As I said, they come from Luton
All I can say, Richard, is that I'm certainly looking forward to the day
When you're going to send me a cassette of more of their material
Because I think they're ace
And before that you hear something from our repeat session by Chevy
The track was called Rock On
It's 28 minutes to midnight on Radio 1
Anne Green, who lives in Oldfield, West Yorkshire
Deserves a mention
Birth date was 9th of March

hope it went well i know what auditions are like frightening hope you got the job hope you got the

because it would be a shame if they had to close. Here's the new single by Saxon.
Saxon, Strangers in the Night. A single, and also on the album Wheels of Steel.
Most especially for Sharon Hardinge, from Dick Clark, Alan Knowles, Val Knowles, Keith Knowles,
Ken Knowles, and Carolyn Hall of Stratum. And also for all the fifth form Edbangers at

Neil and Nellie and Dean and Simon and Spenner and Rick and Dodsey.
From Vince Jennings.
Gotta mention, Dave Harrop, Tudor, and all the Saxon militia everywhere.
Best wishes from Mark Stoneman, who lives in Stourport upon Severn.
Know it well, in Worcestershire.
Brother Thomas, that's the brother of Amanda Knight in Plymouth.
And Dad, Dad, that is Mr. Knight, up in Edinburgh.
Amanda's thinking of him.
And we close with Sweet Savage.
I do hope somebody gives them a good record deal.
They deserve it.
Sweet Savage, one of our session bands tonight,
and Killing Time.
And that's it for this edition of the Friday Rock Show.
Hello, Debbie.
And hello, Natalie.
Many thanks to Ian and Mike, my technical operators tonight.
Rock on tomorrow, we've got Tom Waits
and also a little tribute to Jeff Beck
and a lot of good music.
That's between 5 and 6.30 tomorrow.
Had a postcard from Tony Wilson this morning.
He's in Nashville, Tennessee.
He's turned on the country music now.
Ho, ho, ho, ho!
We'll come back on.
Hi, Tommy, how you doing?
This programme tonight has been produced by Phil Stannard

I want to repudiate

From me, Tommy Vance in London
